text_clean: One of the world's largest monasteries. Beautiful colours all around. Free moving around the rooms where the nuns lived, and in their religious spaces. One can hire a local guide (and get an entrance ticket) in many world languages at the entrance and get a guided tour. text_clean: It might sound insane to rate this site as 2 star consider is a world heritage site but this was one of the most deflating experiences of my trip. We got the circuito 1 ticket which basically keeps you out of the site and you get is a view. AND NOTICE ONCE UP THERE YOU CANNOT BUY A SECOND TICKET . When we realized this we tried to get another ticket but we were told we needed to go down to the town and buy another one and then go back up again. Which means a couple of hours by bus and your train tickets might be lost as they also have set times. To be honest they should not sell this ticket! thank God they sun came out and we had a site other wise we would have spend $300 to see a cloud! I really dislike how artificial and turistic this attraction is and how circuito 1 feels more like a scam

text_clean: I visited the archaeological site today, and although the tour was interesting, I was shocked by the living standards of the animals on the site, present to illustrate the resources of the ancient people. There were ducks with no access to fresh water, which is part of their essential living environment - instead they had a stagnant, filthy puddle and were surrounded by their own waste. The guinea pigs were also living in tiny pens, covered in their own waste. I would ask the site management to please rethink the provisions they are giving to these animals - perhaps even charging a slightly higher ticket price to allow for a better environment. The current living standards are really not appropriate, and lets down an otherwise good value tour.

text_clean: It was nice, not even close to my favorite thing in Peru or my favorite trek but I would say definitely worth two to three days if you have time and are in the arequipa area. It is a very developed area for tourism so expect it to be busy and to have to pay for camping. I was recommended to take 5 litres of water, I took 6, and I did drink around 4, the reason I am included this however is that there were multiple locations where I could have refilled with my filter but didn't need to, therefore unless you drink a very large amount of water I wouldn't bring 6 litres. This is of course in the part of the canyon I did which started near cabanaconde.

text_clean: Machu Picchu Is undoubtedly one of the most incredible places we've ever been too, helped by good weather and a quieter day with fewer people. However, there's a few things though that could be done to improve the experience, particularly given the hefty price tag attached to the visit. - There is zero information around the site about it's history or design. When visiting one of the wonders of the world it seems insane that zero information is provided to let people learn more about the sites rich past. I presume this is to encourage the use of guides, but regardless a small number of boards, even just indicating what certain spaces are or what they were used for, should be a minimum. - Toilets should be free. Given the amount it costs, there is no excuse for charging for the toilets at the top of the hill. If required, get people to show their tickets before being able to use the bathroom, but to outright charge is just a desperate attempt to squeeze you for a couple more dollars

text_clean: A spectacular canyon, and in my view even more spectacular historical site. The terraces built by the pre-inca civilitation that stretch through the valley are stunning. I cannot begin to imagine the amount of labour and sheer effort that went into building these. There are some excellent opportunities to take short hikes to scenic viewpoints, and inbetween you get around easily by bus or car, so it's very well suited for the not so outdoor-minded among us, which includes me. There is also an active volcanoe that erupts from time to time, making for a spectacular view.

text_clean: I know, I know.. you’d think I’d give a place like Machu Picchu more than 3 stars… but honestly I’ve been here twice and, while definitely dramatic looking, unless you seriously love Incan culture or are willing to pay for a guide, you won’t get much out of the experience other than Instagram content… Speaking of which, if you want to avoid getting a million people in your photos you have GOT to pick the first slots of the morning (06:00a), I can’t stress this enough… don’t go after 10am unless you lovvvvvve huge crowds, heat, and long queues. The Peruvian government is keeping some of the silly covid changes in place, namely the unidirectional walking path and new “circuits” method of ticketing where they’ve split the park up and you have to purchase multiple tickets to see the whole thing, an obvious money grab at this point and another reason I knocked off a star.

The Peruvian government is keeping some of the silly covid changes in place, namely the unidirectional walking path and new “circuits” method of ticketing where they’ve split the park up and you have to purchase multiple tickets to see the whole thing, an obvious money grab at this point and another reason I knocked off a star.
label: neutro

text_clean: Arequipa is an incredible experience overall and surprisingly affordable! Known as the White City, Arequipa boasts charming architecture and a vibrant culture that makes every visit special. One of the highlights of my trip was visiting the breathtaking Colca Canyon. The landscapes of Colca Canyon are absolutely unbelievable. The vast, rugged beauty of the canyon leaves you in awe, and it's a fantastic place to connect with nature. The tranquility and peacefulness you find there are unmatched. It's a perfect escape from the hustle and bustle of daily life. Walking along the canyon, you can truly appreciate the serene environment and the stunning views that stretch as far as the eye can see. Watching the majestic condors glide through the air is an experience in itself. Arequipa adds to the experience with its unique blend of historical sites and modern amenities. The combination of the city's unique charm and the breathtaking beauty of Colca Canyon makes for a memorable visit. Whether you're hiking the trails, soaking in the hot springs, or simply taking in the views, it's a place that invites you to relax and unwind. Overall, visiting Colca Canyon in Arequipa is an unforgettable experience that I highly recommend. The natural beauty, peaceful atmosphere, and affordability make it a must-see destination for anyone looking to explore the wonders of Peru.

text_clean: Fui al circuito 1-C el 17 de abril a las 8:00 am y me tocó lluvia y niebla. No obstante despeja por momentos y se puede apreciar la vista desde la casa del guardian al que se tiene acceso, pero por la condición climática descarté la subida que ofrecía la ruta porque no valía la pena. Mejor reservar para entrar al medio día, vas a la fija. Aunque el paisaje es encantador por las montañas, incluso desde el tren, me pareció que tanto sacrificio y dinero para llegar hasta allí está un poco sobrevalorado. Demasiados turistas.

text_clean: The actual canyon is breathtaking, this rating is for the local tourism board. We spent a couple of days in Colca Canyon, and all of a sudden on the second day there was a road block where they demanded 70 soles per person tourism tax. We argued with the guy that if you want to charge tourists extortionate amounts of money just to drive through the canyon, you need to let people know before they enter so that one can make an informed decision. I don't really have a problem with paying, I have a problem with the dishonesty. When I complained that there wasn't even a sign, the guy ran around his little hut and got the sign out. He also didn't speak a single word of English, despite the fact that his job is literally to only deal with foreign tourists (locals don't have to pay). When I asked what the money was for, we were told that we get a paper map, and tourist infrastructure. I don't need a paper map, and usually tourist infrastructure is built so that tourists come into the area and then spend money. We will have spent a hell of a lot of money in accomodation, food, car hire, fuel, souvenirs, ... all of which benefits the local economy and through taxes the local government. Why are we asked to pay extra when they don't even provide free public toilets anywhere, and without being information of the cost before entering?

text_clean: The guards act irresponsibly and put the guests' health at risk by taking away their water. Attached is a photo of the entrance where this happend. We did Circuit 3 and got through the entrance of circuit 3/4 without any problems. When we entered again (through the entrance to the Mountain, which is also for Circuit 1+2) our backpack was suddenly too big. We had (for 2 people) 3l of water in a drinking system built into the backpack, 2 breads in a foldable can, sunscreen and 2 rain jackets. So nothing big, no plastic waste and our backpack fit in the small model box. We discussed in Spanish whether they really wanted to take away our water for the strenuous hike up the mountain in the midday heat, but they remained adamant. This irresponsible behavior inevitably leads to dehydration, which is dangerous since there is no way to buy more water. Otherwise the purchased ticket will be useless. I am horrified by the conditions there and how carelessly they provoke medical emergencies.

text_clean: The site is amazing and well worth a visit, but you need to be aware about a few "roadblocks". Tickets can only be booked online. On site they are only selling cable car tickets. There is only one poor person to help you with the entrance buying process managed by the Ministery of Culture. Once you finally have all your tickets, you need to wait for the bus to take you to the cable car station (10min), and then there are 20min of a very scenic trip before reaching the path leading to the site (30mins walk, horses available). Once you reached the site, there are guides but you better speak Spanish :-) There is a guide who learned a little English and he is doing his best but communication will be difficult. That being said, do not miss i!!. We don't know much about the people who built this place because Incas conquered the city 70 years before Conquistadors burned it, and the site is extremely interesting.
